evidence: Package hierarchy follows a clean top-down direction: api → exploration/query → config → model → utils. No reverse imports observed.
LongIntHashMap (utils) is a self-contained leaf; it imports only java.util.Arrays — zero coupling outward.
The api package imports from exploration, query, config, and model — all downstream. No upstream pull.
exploration imports from query, config, and model but never from api — direction is maintained.
CollocateQueryHelper is package-private inside query/, functioning as an internal helper without leaking out.
WordSketchApiServer is the wiring hub for all handler classes; changes to HttpApiUtils or a shared handler pattern have blast radius across the entire api/ package (10 classes).
RadialPlot (rendering) and ExploreResponseBuilder (presentation translation) coexist with HTTP handlers in api/ — three distinct concerns in one flat package, all importing from each other's layer.
No import cycles detected across packages.

Package decomposition maps to domain capabilities: config/ for grammar loading, model/ for domain types, api/ for HTTP, exploration/ for semantic algorithms, query/ for corpus access, utils/ for shared primitives.
GrammarConfig and GrammarConfigLoader are cleanly separated — the loader owns file I/O, the config owns the data. Callers accept GrammarConfig directly without needing the loader.
model/ package is self-contained: it has no imports from api/ or exploration/. It is a pure domain package.
The handler split (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, BcqlHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) correctly separates endpoint concerns so each file owns a coherent surface.

RadialPlot in api/ was moved in r17 to co-locate with VisualizationHandlers, but RadialPlot generates SVG and has no HTTP-specific logic — its ownership story blurs the 'api/' contract for a new engineer.

QueryResults resides in model/ but is produced by query/ and consumed most heavily by api/ and exploration/ — the package signal (model/) says 'domain data' but the production origin is 'query output'.
exploration/ and query/ are separate packages with no coupling between them: exploration consumes query results but doesn't directly invoke query logic.

QueryExecutor interface has a single production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor). The Javadoc explicitly acknowledges this: 'acts primarily as a testability seam rather than an abstraction boundary.' This is a valid use of an interface.

SemanticFieldExplorer is a thin public facade: exploreMultiSeed() delegates entirely to MultiSeedExplorer.explore(); compareCollocateProfiles() delegates entirely to CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles(). The class owns only the single-seed algorithm and the guard logic.
ExploreResponseBuilder is a focused translation class with no duplication. It owns all model-to-JSON conversion for exploration responses.
HttpApiUtils contains cohesive, small helpers (sendJsonResponse, sendError, parseQueryParams, requireParam, wrapWithErrorHandling) — not a dumping ground.

CollocateQueryHelper is a package-private extraction that owns collocate scoring and ranking shared across 4 BlackLabQueryExecutor methods. This is a genuine extraction of shared logic, not a pass-through.

The deprecated SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) and CollocateProfileComparator(QueryExecutor) constructors still create bypass code paths that circumvent GrammarConfig — they are live dead-weight, not testability seams.
The deprecated 5-param exploreMultiSeed() delegates to the same internal method as the preferred ExploreOptions overload — pure pass-through wrapper, no added logic.

pom.xml runtime dependencies: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser (same library ecosystem — two intentional modules), fastjson2, slf4j-api, logback-classic, jspecify. Total 6 production dependencies — lean.
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose: one JSON library (fastjson2), one logging facade (slf4j), one logging impl (logback), one annotation library (jspecify).
HTTP server uses com.sun.net.httpserver — a JDK built-in that requires no pom.xml entry. Avoids adding a web framework dependency.

fastjson2 (Alibaba, com.alibaba.fastjson2) is used for all JSON serialization and deserialization throughout the codebase. This library had a series of security vulnerabilities in its fastjson v1 predecessor; fastjson2 is a complete rewrite but carries lower community vetting than Jackson or Gson in the Java ecosystem.

com.sun.net.httpserver is a com.sun.* internal API. In Java 18+ it was stabilized via JEP 408 as a simple web server, but the programmatic API (HttpServer.create) was always accessible since Java 6. The risk is lower than typical com.sun usage.
No unused dependencies visible from source — every declared dependency is imported somewhere.
jspecify 1.0.0 is used only for @Nullable / @NonNull annotations and has minimal transitive footprint.

MultiSeedExplorer.explore() is split into 4 named phase helpers (fetchCollocatesPerSeed, identifyCommonCollocates, buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates) — clean decomposition, each helper is focused.
SemanticFieldExplorer has the same 4-phase helper pattern (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) — consistent style across related classes.
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses a BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String, Object>> builder callback to unify two dispatch paths cleanly.

parseDoubleParam returns -1.0 as an error sentinel. Callers check 'if (minLogDice < 0) return null'. This creates an overloaded signal: -1.0 means both 'parse error' and (hypothetically) a legitimate negative threshold.
parseIntParam returns -1 as error sentinel for the same reason — consistent but fragile.

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples parses the 'top' parameter with its own inline try/catch (Integer.parseInt) instead of using parseIntParam from ExplorationHandlers — parseIntParam is private there, forcing duplication.

GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition walks pattern tokens twice: once via CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens() and once with a manual cursor scan over the raw string. The dual traversal is cognitively expensive.

BcqlHandlers.parseBcqlRequest uses nullable boxing (Integer top = obj.get('top') != null ? ... : null; if (top == null) top = ...) — the chained null checks are workable but slightly cluttered compared to a direct int with default.
CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits is ~70 lines including null-guards for conc==null and parts==null — the defensive guards are appropriate for a deserialization boundary but add line count.
ExplorationHandlers → ExploreResponseBuilder seam is clean: handlers deal only with HttpExchange + params; ExploreResponseBuilder handles all model-to-JSON translation. No scattered mapping code.
ValidatedExploreRequest record bundles pre-validated state (seedsRaw, relationConfig, exploreParams) so handler methods don't re-extract parameters inline.
GrammarConfigLoader → GrammarConfig seam is clean: loader owns I/O and parsing; config owns querying. Handlers accept GrammarConfig — no loader reference escapes the startup wiring.

handleSemanticFieldComparison does not use validateExploreRequest (it doesn't need a RelationConfig) but reimplements ExploreParams resolution by calling parseIntParam('top') and parseDoubleParam('min_logdice') directly — minor seam inconsistency.

parseIntParam and parseDoubleParam are private to ExplorationHandlers but ConcordanceHandlers needs the same pattern, causing it to re-implement inline parsing with its own try/catch. The parameter-parsing seam is not properly bounded.
ExploreOptions record is the parameter carrier across the api→exploration boundary — clean, minimal, named fields.
SemanticFieldExplorer is a clean injection point: ExplorationHandlers accepts SemanticFieldExplorer (not BlackLabQueryExecutor directly), so test doubles can be injected without standing up BlackLab.
Model-layer types (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult) are plain data objects with no HTTP concerns — the boundary is clean in both directions.

Comments throughout explain WHY, not what. Example: 'Use explicit relation_type filter rather than findFirst() to avoid implicit ordering dependency.' (CollocateProfileComparator). Example: 'Over-fetch factor: request 3x as many hits as needed to compensate for scoring discards.' (CollocateQueryHelper).
No entry/exit logging on every function. Logging is targeted: debug-level for algorithm state, warn-level for fallback paths, info-level for startup.

Two of the 11 routes in WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes() have an inline logger.info('Received request: {}', exchange.getRequestURI()) — the other 9 routes have no such log. This is inconsistent low-value logging rather than systematic noise.

ExplorationHandlers constructor: 'if (grammarConfig == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("grammarConfig must not be null; ...") }' — verbose null-guard with a long message. WordSketchApiServer uses Objects.requireNonNull for the same purpose. Minor inconsistency.
Deprecated constructors (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) have clear, purposeful @Deprecated + migration notes — not boilerplate.
No restating comments found (e.g., '// iterate over relations' above a for loop).
No pass-through wrapper functions with no added logic (SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed is deprecated + documented as a migration artifact).
No generic domain names (handleData, processItem) — domain terms used throughout (seed, collocate, lemma, logDice, concordance).
RadialPlot has many named constants (SPIRAL_START_RADIUS, SPIRAL_RADIUS_STEP, etc.) — these are legitimate documentation of magic layout values, not AI-debt.

Three deprecated items exist with 'Scheduled for removal in v2.0': SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) constructor, CollocateProfileComparator(QueryExecutor) constructor, SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed(Set, RelationConfig, double, int, int) 5-param overload.
The deprecated constructors are documented with clear migration paths (use the 2-param GrammarConfig-accepting constructor).
The deprecated 5-param exploreMultiSeed is documented: 'Prefer exploreMultiSeed(Set, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions)' — migration target is clear.
BcqlHandlers: 'The deprecated limit field is also accepted for backward-compat alias' — documented single-field deprecated alias in the POST body. The canonical field is 'top'.
No mixed language migrations (pure Java codebase).
No stale migration TODOs referencing 'legacy', 'old api', or 'remove after X date' found in source.
No evidence of external consumers requiring backward-compat preservation — this is an internal API.
ExploreOptions.forMultiSeed() factory was added in r17 as the target; all call sites updated per the r17 changelog — migration is tracked in changelog.

Package census: api/ (10 files), config/ (4 files), exploration/ (4 files), indexer/blacklab/ (2 files), model/ (9 files), query/ (4 files), utils/ (3 files), root (Main.java). Total ~37 production files across 8 packages.

model/ holds both semantic-domain types (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, Edge) and query-layer output types (QueryResults). These have different production origins and change drivers.
QueryResults is produced by QueryExecutor (query/ package) and consumed by api/ and exploration/ — its import affinity is with query/ (same package as its producer) not model/.
api/ package has 10 files: 5 HTTP handlers, 1 server, 1 HTTP utility, 1 response builder, 1 renderer (RadialPlot), 1 route wiring. Three distinct concerns share a flat namespace.
RadialPlot generates SVG with spiral layout constants (BASELINE_CANVAS_SIZE, SPIRAL_START_RADIUS, etc.) — a rendering component, not an HTTP adapter.
config/ has 4 cohesive files (GrammarConfig, GrammarConfigLoader, RelationConfig, RelationUtils) — well-sized and focused.
exploration/ has 4 cohesive files (SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, ExploreOptions) — well-sized.
utils/ has 3 files (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap) — cohesive utility package with low coupling.
Main.java at root package is appropriate as the CLI entry point — high fan-in to all subsystems, not a straggler.
QueryExecutor interface Javadoc specifies pre/post conditions for all 6 methods with precision (pattern format constraints, null behavior, logDice semantics). The implementation honors them.
HttpApiUtils.requireParam is annotated @Nullable and documented: 'Caller must check for null return.' This is honored by all callers (pattern: 'if (x == null) return;').

parseDoubleParam returns -1.0 on parse failure; resolveExploreParams checks 'if (minLogDice < 0) return null'. The contract works for logDice (which is 0-14) but is implicit: there is no documented precondition that minLogDice must be non-negative, and a caller passing -1.0 deliberately would silently get a 400.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() wraps all IOExceptions (including FileNotFoundException for config-not-found, malformed JSON, etc.) in a single IllegalStateException — callers cannot distinguish missing file from corrupt config without parsing the message string.

RelationConfig.buildFullPattern(String headword) returns the unmodified pattern silently when headword is null or blank — callers may not realize the substitution was skipped, leading to unsubstituted patterns in queries.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles public wrapper checks 'seedNouns == null || seedNouns.size() < 2' and throws IllegalArgumentException. The private CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles checks 'seedNouns == null || seedNouns.isEmpty()' — the guards are slightly different (isEmpty vs size < 2), though the public layer ensures the weaker guard is always covered first.
ExplorationResult.empty(String seed) contract: '@param seed must not be null' and throws NPE via Objects.requireNonNull — honored.
handleConcordanceExamples returns early via explicit 'return' after error sends — consistent null-return-after-error-send pattern used throughout all handlers.

SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType: the if-guard `rel.relationType().orElse(null) == relationType` already confirms the Optional is non-empty, yet the immediately following `rel.relationType().orElseThrow(...)` can never actually throw — the orElseThrow is vacuously safe and introduces unnecessary verbosity

ExplorationHandlers.parseIntParam and parseDoubleParam use -1 as error sentinel; callers check `< 0` to detect failure, conflating 'parse error' with 'negative value'. A caller passing `top=0` passes validation silently with value 0, which will produce no results without an error response

ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams: parseDoubleParam returns -1 (int literal promoted to double) on failure; `if (minLogDice < 0) return null` catches it correctly, but a user who genuinely passes min_logdice=-0.1 (nonsensical but not outright malformed) would receive a null result treated identically to a parse error, without a distinct error message

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern: the null/empty check `if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty())` correctly guards before the `seed.toLowerCase()` reassignment — no dead-code or logic inversion issue

WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes: the `/api/semantic-field/explore` and `/api/semantic-field/explore-multi` handler lambdas add a logger.info call inline before delegating, while the other handlers use plain method references — minor inconsistency in dispatch style but no logic defect

Main.handleBlackLabQueryCommand: `executor.findCollocations(lemma, "[]", minLogDice, limit)` passes `[]` as the CQL pattern; buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution checks `cqlPattern.startsWith("[")` which is satisfied, producing `"lemma" []`, which is a syntactically valid 'match any token' query — logic is correct but the pattern is semantically opaque
QueryResults.CollocateResult.collocateLemma is annotated @Nullable — correct, recently added
HttpApiUtils.requireParam is annotated @Nullable — callers must null-check the return value
BlackLabSnippetParser methods that can return null are annotated @Nullable (extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, extractHeadword, extractPlainTextTokenAt)

CollocateQueryHelper.extractCollocateLemma(String matchXml, int collocatePos) returns String (not @Nullable) but can return null when BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateLemma(matchXml) returns null — the method's return type is missing the @Nullable annotation

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore calls resolvedConfig.relationType().get() without isPresent() guard, relying on the contract that resolveRelationConfig already validated the field — safe by convention but the type system provides no enforcement

CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex with a comment 'Null is accepted to support test subclasses'; production callers must always supply non-null — the @Nullable annotation is correct but the split contract is implicit
RelationConfig.getDeprel() returns @Nullable String — good; callers in SketchHandlers check `rel.getDeprel() != null` before using it
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses a BiFunction callback typed as BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String,Object>> — fully typed, no raw types
Map<String,Object> is the universal response type throughout the API layer — unavoidable with the hand-rolled JSON serialization approach, but means the type system cannot verify response shapes

BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectFrequenciesFromGroups accepts a Function<String,String> lambda — the function can return null (documented by callers checking for null), but the Function type parameter is not annotated with @Nullable on the return side

RelationConfig.getDeprel() uses a 'get' prefix but performs non-trivial computation: compiles a regex, runs a Matcher, and falls back to substring parsing of the relation ID — the 'get' prefix implies O(1) field access, misleading callers

SemanticFieldExplorer.filterCandidates — 'candidates' is vague; the method filters DiscoveredNoun candidates by minShared threshold; a name like filterByMinShared or scoreAndFilterNouns would communicate the criterion

CollocateQueryHelper.CollocateSearch — the record holds a headword frequency and HitGroups result, but 'Search' suggests the in-progress action, not the result; CollocateSearchResult or CollocateHitData would be clearer
VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial — 'Visual' is redundant in a class already named VisualizationHandlers; handleRadialPlot or handleRadial would be more concise

ExploreResponseBuilder — the '*Builder' suffix in Java strongly implies a fluent builder pattern (returning this); this class is a static utility formatter (private constructor, all-static methods); ExploreResponseFormatter or ExploreResponseSerializer would better match the Java convention

ExplorationHandlers.buildBaseExploreResponse — 'Base' is accurate (it builds shared structure), but the distinction between 'base' and 'full' response is only apparent after reading the method body; buildSharedExploreResponseFrame would be slightly more informative

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern — the 5-param package-private overload and the 3-param public overload share a name; the 5-param overload is an implementation detail exposed as a package-private method, creating a minor vocabulary ambiguity
ExploreOptions.forMultiSeed — good factory name; the asymmetry in 'Explore' vs 'Exploration' (ExplorationResult) is acknowledged in Javadoc as intentional
All HTTP handlers are wrapped by HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling: IllegalArgumentException → 400, IOException → 500, Exception → 500 — consistent error boundary at the API layer
ExplorationHandlers extracts parseIntParam/parseDoubleParam as named helpers that return a sentinel (-1) and call sendError internally — parse errors produce explicit 400 responses

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples uses an inline try/catch for NumberFormatException on the 'top' parameter, duplicating the pattern that ExplorationHandlers factored into parseIntParam — structural inconsistency across sibling handlers for the same error category

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries catches `IOException | RuntimeException` per relation and accumulates errors in a list rather than propagating, returning partial results — documented 'partial success' strategy, intentional boundary

BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor wraps any Exception from BlackLab.open as IOException with message context preserved: `throw new IOException("Failed to open index: " + e.getMessage(), e)` — good chaining

CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency wraps RuntimeException as IOException: `throw new IOException("Unexpected failure retrieving frequency for lemma '" + lemma + "'", e)` — good context and chaining

BcqlHandlers wraps JSONException as IllegalArgumentException (→ 400): `throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid JSON in request body: " + e.getMessage(), e)` — correct and consistent with VisualizationHandlers doing the same
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand: grammar config load failure logs with error level, prints to stderr, closes executor explicitly, and returns — no exception propagation, clean shutdown path
ExplorationHandlers constructor throws IllegalArgumentException (not IllegalStateException) for null grammarConfig — appropriate for a precondition violation
SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles throws IllegalArgumentException for fewer-than-2 seeds — consistent with the IllegalArgumentException-for-preconditions pattern used throughout

HttpApiUtils.getCorsAllowOrigin() reads System.getProperty at call time, not at class load — the Javadoc explicitly notes this was moved from a static final field to a method to avoid the stale-snapshot problem; this is a correct design decision
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor performs I/O (BlackLab.open) — declared throws IOException making the side effect explicit; no hidden I/O at class load time
WordSketchApiServer constructor builds handler objects but defers port binding to start() — a comment explicitly documents this separation of concerns
All static final regex patterns in BlackLabSnippetParser are compiled at class load time — safe, immutable, no ordering dependency
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES is a static Map.of(...) at class load — immutable, no dependency on other modules at load time

SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator both have deprecated no-GrammarConfig constructors that fall back to hardcoded pattern constants — these create a parallel initialization code path but are documented as deprecated-for-removal; production use always passes GrammarConfig
GrammarConfigLoader.load() is called explicitly from Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand, not at class initialization time — no hidden import-time I/O
ExplorationHandlers constructor validates grammarConfig != null at construction time with IllegalArgumentException — fails fast rather than deferring to first handler call

No circular init dependencies found: the dependency graph is WordSketchApiServer → handlers → SemanticFieldExplorer / QueryExecutor → CollocateQueryHelper → BlackLabIndex, which is a clean acyclic chain
Package structure is consistent: api/, config/, exploration/, indexer/blacklab/, model/, query/, utils/ — clear, stable organization with no outlier directories
Handler classes are uniformly plural *Handlers (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, BcqlHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) — consistent naming within the api package

Model type strategy: simple data carriers are records (AdjectiveProfile, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, Edge, ExploreOptions), while result objects with domain logic are classes (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult) — the split is logical though not documented

Accessor naming: record components use accessor style (adjective(), nounScores()), while class fields use getter style (getSeed(), getSeedCollocates()) — this split follows Java convention but mixes access patterns within the model package

RelationConfig mixes method naming conventions: getDeprel() uses getter prefix, collocateReversePattern() uses noun-phrase style, buildFullPattern() uses verb style — three different naming patterns on one class

ExploreResponseBuilder — '*Builder' suffix in a static utility class is an outlier against the Java convention where *Builder implies a fluent builder with a build() method; all other utility classes avoid this pattern

RadialPlot class is in the api/ package following recent relocation; it serves as a rendering utility that could equally sit in utils/ or viz/ — its placement is consistent with VisualizationHandlers but differs from the utils/ convention for non-HTTP utility code

HttpApiUtils private constructor pattern (private HttpApiUtils() {}) matches CqlUtils (private CqlUtils() {}), LogDiceCalculator (no explicit private constructor but final class), and RelationUtils — mostly consistent static-utility pattern
ConcordanceHandlers uses inline NumberFormatException handling while ExplorationHandlers uses extracted parseIntParam helpers — a structural outlier within the sibling handler classes
222 tests across 18 test classes — reasonable coverage density for a codebase of this size

Unit tests exist for: HttpApiUtils, WrapHandler, HandlersTest, ExploreResponseBuilder, RadialPlot, GrammarConfigLoader, RelationUtils, RelationsValidation, CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, SemanticFieldExplorerInternal, ConlluConverter, BlackLabSnippetParser, CollocateQueryHelper, SemanticFieldExplorer (stub-based), BCQLParser, CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap
e2e/ test directory is empty — no end-to-end tests; the full path from HTTP request to BlackLab query execution to response formatting is not tested in an integrated way
regression/ test directory is empty — no regression test suite protecting against previously fixed bugs
viz/ test directory is empty — but RadialPlotTest exists in api/, so the RadialPlot class is tested
indexer/hybrid/ test directory is empty

BlackLabQueryExecutor is the core query engine (high import count, complex logic) — BlackLabQueryExecutorTest.java exists but uses stub-based testing (BlackLabDummyTest.java) without a real index; critical production paths through executeCollocateSearch and collectHits are not exercised against actual BlackLab behavior
BlackLabConllUIndexer has no test — ConlluConverterTest tests the preprocessing step only; the actual BlackLab indexing step is untested
HandlersTest uses inline MockExchange — reasonable for HTTP unit testing, not coupled to implementation details
SemanticFieldExplorerTest uses StubExecutor correctly: tests algorithmic logic without index dependency — good separation of concerns
No snapshot tests detected — appropriate for a data-processing system where outputs change with corpus

Test-production coupling: HandlersTest directly accesses package-private handler classes (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers) — tests will break on refactoring package structure but are functionally accurate

QueryExecutor interface: all collocate-retrieval methods share a consistent parameter ordering (lemma, pattern/deprel, minLogDice, maxResults) — coherent across findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern
executeDependencyPatternWithPos adds a 5th parameter headPosConstraint at the end — consistent with the 'add optional params last' convention

SemanticFieldExplorer public API: exploreByPattern(seed, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) and exploreMultiSeed(seeds, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) share a parallel signature shape — the ExploreOptions object bundles variable params consistently
Deprecated exploreMultiSeed(seeds, RelationConfig, double, int, int) 5-param overload still exists as public API — a caller browsing the interface sees two methods for the same operation

compareCollocateProfiles(Set<String>, double, int) uses raw parameters while exploreByPattern and exploreMultiSeed use ExploreOptions — inconsistency within the same class's public API; compareCollocateProfiles was not updated to accept ExploreOptions
ExplorationHandlers: all four handler methods accept only HttpExchange — consistent; the shared validation is factored into validateExploreRequest and resolveExploreParams

HTTP API: /api/semantic-field/explore accepts 'seeds' as a single noun while /api/semantic-field/explore-multi requires comma-separated seeds >= 2 — the parameter name is the same ('seeds') but the expected format differs (single vs CSV); no documentation in the URL distinguishes this

HTTP GET parameter 'top' is the canonical limit param across /api/semantic-field/explore, /api/semantic-field/explore-multi, /api/semantic-field, and /api/semantic-field/examples — consistent after the limit→top rename
/api/bcql uses POST+JSON while all exploration endpoints use GET+query-params — justified in comments ('to avoid URL encoding issues') and documented in the server Javadoc class header
ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges is overloaded for ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult — clean use of Java overloading for type dispatch

The server has no authentication mechanism — all endpoints are publicly accessible; this is a consistent design choice for a local read-only research tool bound to loopback (InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress())

The server binds exclusively to the loopback address: `new InetSocketAddress(InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress(), port)` — the network-level isolation replaces application-level auth for the stated use case

Input validation is applied consistently across all endpoints: required parameters are checked via HttpApiUtils.requireParam, numeric parameters are parsed with error responses, and unknown relations receive 400 errors

BcqlHandlers applies additional input validation compared to other endpoints: MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH=1024, MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH=20, MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES=65536 — appropriately stricter for an endpoint that accepts arbitrary query syntax
CQL injection is mitigated: lemma substitution uses CqlUtils.escapeForRegex which escapes backslashes and double-quotes before embedding user input in CQL patterns

CORS headers are applied uniformly via HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader on every response-sending method (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse) — no endpoint can accidentally omit CORS headers

The cors.allow.origin system property defaults to http://localhost:3000 and is documented; production deployments can override it — this is the only access-control mechanism, appropriate for the single-origin use case
No session management, no user identity, no RLS — N/A for this read-only corpus analysis tool
All endpoints treat the corpus index as read-only; no write endpoints exist except the indexer CLI which runs as a separate process
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The absence of any rate limiting or request throttling means the BCQL endpoint could be used to execute expensive corpus queries at high frequency from any process on the same machine; the loopback binding provides no protection against local denial-of-service

evidence: MultiSeedExplorer.explore is cleanly decomposed into 4 named phase helpers (fetchCollocatesPerSeed, identifyCommonCollocates, buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates) each with a focused responsibility and clear name
SemanticFieldExplorer is a facade delegating to MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator — the single-responsibility split is explicit and documented in the class Javadoc

ExplorationHandlers.validateExploreRequest bundles the three-step validation preamble (parse, require seeds, resolve relation, resolve numeric params) into a single ValidatedExploreRequest record — reduces the per-handler setup boilerplate from ~10 lines to 3

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries extracts the shared iteration/execution loop shared by handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType, eliminating duplication via a BiFunction builder callback — good structural refactoring

compareCollocateProfiles is not covered by ExploreOptions — the single exploration class has two different calling conventions (ExploreOptions for explore/multi, raw params for comparison), requiring callers to remember which method takes which form

ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams extracts nounsPerCollocate (only applicable to single-seed) alongside the shared params (top, minShared, minLogDice, nounsPerCollocate), then the multi-seed handler explicitly rejects 'nouns_per' if provided — the shared params object bundles a param that is not shared

WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes contains 11 registerGet/registerPost calls — the route table is data that could be expressed more compactly but is not complex enough to warrant a data-driven route registry; acceptable at current scale

CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits and scoreHits represent a two-phase pipeline within the BCQL query path; the pipeline is clearly named and the record type HitRecord carries exactly what phase 2 needs from phase 1 — good design

SemanticFieldExplorer.deriveNounCqlConstraint has nested stream+orElseGet logic (find OBJECT_OF/SUBJECT_OF → fallback to any NOUN collocate → fallback to constant) — the nesting is justified by the 3-level priority chain but is harder to read than the named-phase pattern used elsewhere

ExploreResponseBuilder (static utility) holds all response-building logic for three different caller paths (explore, multi-seed, comparison) — the consolidation is correct but the class is growing; if a fourth response type is added it may need splitting

strengths: Strict top-down import discipline: no reverse dependencies between packages (api never imported by exploration/query/config).
CollocateQueryHelper is correctly scoped package-private so the boundary does not leak.
LongIntHashMap is a cleanly isolated leaf component with no outward coupling.
exploration/ and query/ are separate packages with no coupling between them, cleanly separating semantic algorithm from corpus access.
The single-root Main.java wires everything at startup and is the only caller of both api and query layers together — an intentionally narrow coupling point.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The main structural concern is the api/ package acting as a flat hub that mixes HTTP transport, presentation translation, and rendering — this is a concentration issue rather than a direction violation.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase has no dependency cycles and enforces a clean top-down direction throughout all packages. The only architectural concern is the api/ package growing to 10 files with three loosely related concerns (HTTP handlers, ExploreResponseBuilder, RadialPlot) sharing the same namespace, widening blast radius for common changes. This is a moderate concentration problem rather than a boundary violation, placing the score in the solid-but-uneven range.

strengths: Top-level package names map to domain capabilities rather than historical accidents (exploration/, query/, config/ are self-explanatory).
GrammarConfig / GrammarConfigLoader separation is a textbook loader/data split — callers hold only what they need.
model/ is a stable, import-free domain package that any module can depend on without pulling in higher-layer concerns.
The handler decomposition in api/ makes it immediately clear which HTTP surface each class owns.
exploration/ and query/ are decoupled — the semantic algorithm does not know about corpus internals.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The two placement issues (RadialPlot in api/, QueryResults in model/) both blur the ownership story for a new engineer — they are isolated misplacements rather than a systemic decomposition problem.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase has a coherent, intentional top-level structure that maps cleanly to domain capabilities. A new engineer can understand why each package exists and roughly predict what it contains. The score is held below 85 by two placement anomalies: RadialPlot (a rendering component) in api/ and QueryResults (a query-layer output type) in model/. These are navigability friction points rather than deep structural failures.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is acknowledged as a testability seam in its own Javadoc — honest about its single-implementation nature.
CollocateQueryHelper is a legitimate extraction of shared scoring/ranking logic used by 4 BlackLabQueryExecutor methods.
ExploreResponseBuilder is a focused, zero-duplication translation layer.
HttpApiUtils holds cohesive, non-redundant utilities — not a kitchen-sink class.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The main abstractions are well-calibrated; the residual issues are two deprecated constructors that create live bypass code paths, and one deprecated pass-through overload — these add indirection without adding policy.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The majority of abstractions earn their cost: the interface provides a testability seam, CollocateQueryHelper eliminates real duplication, and ExploreResponseBuilder centralizes translation. The score is held below 85 by the three deprecated items that remain as active code: two constructors that bypass config (parallel code paths) and one pass-through overload. These are small but real abstraction debt items rather than a systemic problem.

strengths: Dependency set is minimal — 6 production runtime dependencies for a full corpus-search server with HTTP API.
No duplicate libraries for any purpose (single JSON lib, single logging lib).
JDK built-in HTTP server avoids pulling in a web framework dependency.
jspecify adds type annotation capability with near-zero transitive footprint.
blacklab-engine + blacklab-query-parser are two modules of the same ecosystem — not duplicate purpose.
dependency_health.issue_character: The dependency set is lean and well-chosen; the two minor concerns (fastjson2 audit history, com.sun HTTP server) are low-risk in practice rather than active defects.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The codebase shows clear dependency discipline: minimal set, no duplicates, no over-pulling of heavy libs for light use. The score is held below 90 by the use of fastjson2 (lower ecosystem vetting than Jackson) and reliance on a historically internal JDK API (com.sun.net.httpserver). Neither is a blocking issue today, but both carry non-zero future risk.

strengths: 4-phase helper decomposition in both MultiSeedExplorer and SemanticFieldExplorer produces focused, named steps with clear algorithmic intent.
executeRelationQueries uses a BiFunction callback to avoid duplicating the relation-loop body — clean abstraction.
LongIntHashMap internals are precise: sentinel value documented, linear probing correct, rehash clears state before re-insertion.
ExploreResponseBuilder methods are small, direct, and stateless — each formats exactly one model type.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are localized craft problems in the HTTP parameter parsing layer: the -1 sentinel conflation, a parsing helper that is private to the wrong class, and a dual-traversal in config loading — they form a coherent cluster of similar problems rather than isolated incidents.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The exploration algorithm code shows disciplined extraction and consistent style. The parameter-parsing layer is where low-level elegance drops: parseDoubleParam uses a sentinel return value that conflates error with valid input, the helper is inaccessible to sibling classes, and ConcordanceHandlers duplicates the pattern inline. These are moderate craft defects across a small but high-traffic code path.

strengths: ExploreResponseBuilder centralises all model-to-JSON translation for exploration endpoints — zero scattered mapping code.
ValidatedExploreRequest record makes the 'parse + validate' phase explicit and reusable across three handlers.
GrammarConfigLoader / GrammarConfig separation keeps I/O concerns out of the handlers completely.
SemanticFieldExplorer injection seam supports testing without a live corpus index.
ExploreOptions carries the api→exploration parameter boundary cleanly with named components.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The mid-level issues are localized to the parameter-parsing seam: numeric param helpers are private to one handler class but needed by siblings, and one handler partially re-implements validation inline rather than using the shared preamble.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The major handoffs (api↔exploration, api↔config, exploration↔query) are well-choreographed with clear translation points. The score is held below 85 by the parameter-parsing seam leak: parseIntParam/parseDoubleParam are private to ExplorationHandlers but semantically belong at the HttpApiUtils boundary, causing ConcordanceHandlers to re-implement inline and handleSemanticFieldComparison to partially bypass the validated-request preamble.

strengths: Comments throughout explain non-obvious constraints and design decisions (why algorithm choices were made, not just what the code does).
Logging is targeted and graduated (debug for hot paths, warn for fallbacks, info for startup) — no entry/exit logging on every method.
Deprecated constructors carry clear migration rationale rather than generic 'use X instead' boilerplate.
Domain vocabulary is used consistently (seed, collocate, logDice, concordance, lemma) — no generic handleData or processItem names.
Named constants in RadialPlot document layout parameters rather than hiding magic numbers.
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The two identified issues are isolated inconsistencies (two out of eleven routes log at info vs. nine that don't; one constructor uses manual null check vs. Objects.requireNonNull elsewhere) rather than a systematic AI-debt pattern.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase shows clear authorship intent: comments explain why, not what; logging is disciplined; naming is domain-specific. The score stays below 90 due to two minor inconsistencies — the selective per-route info logging (two routes out of eleven) and the manual null-guard inconsistency — which are easily fixed cosmetic issues rather than substantive debt.

strengths: Every deprecated item has a clear migration path documented in its @Deprecated Javadoc.
Migration targets are concrete and already implemented (forMultiSeed factory, GrammarConfig-accepting constructors).
The BcqlHandlers 'limit' alias is documented inline and proxies to the canonical 'top' parameter without a code-path split.
No stale TODO/FIXME referencing migration found.
ExploreOptions factory migration tracked in changelog — evidence of active migration management.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The three deprecated items are all in the exploration subsystem, following the same pattern (old signature bypasses config), and are intentional rather than forgotten — the migration is in progress, not stalled.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The migration hygiene is good: deprecations are documented, have clear targets, and the r17 changelog shows active tracking. The score is held below 90 because three deprecated items are simultaneously active with no firm removal schedule beyond 'v2.0', which has no committed date. The coexistence of the old 5-param overload alongside the ExploreOptions overload is the clearest incompleteness — the migration target exists and is fully functional.

strengths: Package names map directly to subsystem responsibilities (exploration/, query/, config/, utils/) — easy to navigate.
Small, cohesive packages: config/ (4), exploration/ (4), query/ (4), utils/ (3) are all appropriately sized.
indexer/blacklab/ uses a sub-package for the BlackLab-specific indexer — correct use of hierarchy.
Main.java at the root is appropriate — it is the entry point with high fan-in, not a straggler.
package_organization.issue_character: Two placement issues both follow the same pattern: a file was added to the most convenient existing package (model/ for QueryResults, api/ for RadialPlot) rather than its ownership-correct home.
package_organization.score_rationale: The package structure is generally clean and navigable; a new engineer can locate any subsystem quickly. The score is held below 85 by two placement issues: QueryResults in model/ (production origin is query/) and RadialPlot in api/ (rendering concern, not HTTP transport). Both affect navigability and change-boundary clarity for future developers. The api/ package at 10 files with three distinct concerns is approaching overload territory but is not yet actionable on its own.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface Javadoc is thorough and accurate — all method contracts are explicitly stated and honored by BlackLabQueryExecutor.
HttpApiUtils.requireParam @Nullable annotation and documented null-return contract is consistently honored by all 5 callers.
ExplorationResult.empty() has an explicit non-null precondition enforced by Objects.requireNonNull.
Handler early-return pattern after sendError is consistent across all handler classes — predictable failure mode.
contract_coherence.issue_character: The contract issues are concentrated in three places: a sentinel-overloaded return value (parseDoubleParam), a silent pass-through on substitution failure (buildFullPattern), and a loss of IOException subtype information (createDefaultEnglish). These are genuine contract imprecisions rather than widespread violations.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The core interfaces and domain types have precise, honored contracts. The issues are in the parameter-parsing and config-loading layers where contract precision was traded for brevity. parseDoubleParam's -1.0 sentinel is the most actionable — it conflates two meanings on a single return type. The buildFullPattern silent pass-through is a hazard for callers who don't read the source. These are moderate contract defects in specific code paths, not systemic failures.

strengths: Early-return guard clauses used consistently throughout handlers: `if (req == null) return` and `if (v == null) return` patterns prevent deep nesting
Phase-helper decomposition in SemanticFieldExplorer and MultiSeedExplorer makes multi-step algorithms readable as named sequential steps
Boolean expressions are simple and direct: no `if x: return True` anti-patterns; conditions are short and one-directional
The fetchSeedCollocates fallback path is clearly structured: try BCQL first, log if empty, fall back to simple pattern — the control flow matches the documentation
Null/empty guards on lemma parameters in BlackLabQueryExecutor log the skip reason and return emptyList, avoiding silent failures
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues are isolated micro-level logic annoyances — one redundant Optional orElseThrow and a sentinel-value conflation — rather than systemic control-flow defects.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase is logically clean at the macro level: phase decomposition is clear, guard clauses are consistent, and control flow matches the documented intent across all explored files. The two identified issues (vacuous orElseThrow in handleRelationsForType, -1 sentinel conflation in param parsing) are genuine but local clarity regressions rather than correctness bugs. Against a global anchor where 80 represents 'solid but with a few rough edges', this codebase lands comfortably at 80 — a developer can follow the logic throughout with only minor head-scratching moments.

strengths: @Nullable annotations from jspecify are applied on public-facing methods where null is a documented return possibility (requireParam, extractLemmaFromMatch, extractCollocateLemma, getDeprel)
Records are used for all simple data carriers (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, Edge, AdjectiveProfile, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate) providing compile-time field presence guarantees
QueryExecutor interface is fully typed with explicit IOException declarations on every method — error contracts are machine-checkable
Optional<RelationType> on RelationConfig.relationType() correctly models a genuinely optional field rather than using null, preventing accidental unguarded access
type_safety.issue_character: Type annotation coverage is partial: the codebase applies @Nullable on public API entry points but misses several internal helper return types, creating an incomplete null-safety contract that developers cannot fully rely on.
type_safety.score_rationale: Java's static type system handles most correctness automatically through record types and explicit generics. The jspecify @Nullable annotations are applied selectively and correctly at the public-API boundary but have gaps in internal helpers (CollocateQueryHelper.extractCollocateLemma being the clearest example). The Map<String,Object> response type is an architectural choice rather than a defect. Against a global anchor where 75 represents 'significant drag', this codebase sits slightly above that — the applied annotations are correct, but the incomplete coverage means developers cannot rely on the annotations as a complete null-safety contract.

strengths: Phase helper methods in MultiSeedExplorer and SemanticFieldExplorer have precise verb-noun names that match their algorithmic role (fetchCollocatesPerSeed, identifyCommonCollocates, buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates)
Handler classes follow a consistent *Handlers plural convention (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, BcqlHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers)
Record field names in model types are precise domain vocabulary: collocateLemma, seedCollocates, combinedRelevanceScore, commonalityScore, distinctivenessScore
ExploreOptions.nounsPerCollocate is well-named after the recent rename from nounsPerSeed — it correctly describes the reverse-lookup phase count
naming_quality.issue_character: Issues are naming-behavior mismatches concentrated in utility and builder classes: methods that compute non-trivially but carry accessor naming, and a class name that invokes a Java design pattern (Builder) that the class does not implement.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase's domain vocabulary is rich and consistent — exploration concepts (seed, collocate, logDice, discovery, core) are named uniformly throughout. The identified issues are real: RelationConfig.getDeprel() violates the getter convention by performing regex computation, and ExploreResponseBuilder misrepresents its pattern. These are not isolated to one file but affect multiple callers who must understand the naming gap. Against a 80-anchor of 'solid but uneven', a score of 77.5 reflects a codebase with strong domain naming but a cluster of class and method names that violate Java naming conventions.

strengths: Uniform API error boundary via wrapWithErrorHandling: all 11 registered routes share the same error-to-HTTP-status mapping strategy
Exception wrapping with context is used consistently throughout the query layer: IOException wrappers preserve the original exception and add the failing lemma or pattern to the message
JSON parse errors are consistently re-thrown as IllegalArgumentException (which the wrapper maps to 400) in both BcqlHandlers and VisualizationHandlers
IllegalArgumentException is used for precondition violations consistently: null checks in constructors, missing seeds, unsupported POS groups
error_consistency.issue_character: The main inconsistency is a single structural gap: ConcordanceHandlers duplicates the number-parsing error pattern that ExplorationHandlers already extracted into a reusable helper, creating two slightly different representations of the same error category.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The error strategy is coherent: exceptions flow upward to the wrapWithErrorHandling boundary, which maps them consistently to HTTP status codes. Error context is well-preserved at I/O boundaries. The single identified deviation (ConcordanceHandlers inline NumberFormatException handling vs the extracted parseIntParam helper in ExplorationHandlers) is a real structural inconsistency but a contained one. Against a 75 anchor of 'significant drag', this codebase sits just below that — the main error strategy is sound, and only the sibling-handler inconsistency pulls the score down from the low 80s.

strengths: Port binding explicitly deferred to start() in WordSketchApiServer with a documenting comment — construction and network binding are cleanly separated
All I/O (index open, config file load) occurs at explicit call sites in Main, not at class or static-block initialization time
HttpApiUtils CORS value correctly read at call time rather than class load, preventing the stale-snapshot problem
All dependencies are passed via constructor injection (executor, grammarConfig, semanticFieldExplorer) — no hidden singleton lookups or static service locators
ExplorationHandlers validates its required grammarConfig argument at construction time, failing fast rather than at first request
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The codebase has minimal initialization coupling issues; the main observation is the deprecated fallback constructors in SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator that maintain a parallel hardcoded-pattern code path.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Java's class initialization model and this codebase's explicit dependency injection approach mean there are essentially no import-time side effects or boot-order surprises. The one pattern worth noting — deprecated fallback constructors with hardcoded patterns — is properly marked and documented. Against an 80-anchor of 'solid but uneven', this codebase scores above that: initialization is clean throughout, with the deferred-start pattern in the server being especially well-handled. The deprecated constructors are the only residual coupling concern, holding the score back from 90+.

strengths: Package boundaries are stable and consistent: every package has a clear, single responsibility and no files are misplaced relative to the package theme
All HTTP handler classes follow the plural *Handlers naming convention — a new developer can predict where to find handler code
The records-for-data, classes-for-logic split in the model package is consistently applied even without documentation
Static utility classes uniformly use private constructors to prevent instantiation (HttpApiUtils, CqlUtils, RelationUtils)
convention_outlier.issue_character: Convention outliers are concentrated in naming mismatches rather than structural organization: a Builder-named static utility, getter-named computational methods, and inconsistent naming convention within a single class.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase's structural organization (packages, handler patterns, model type strategy) is highly consistent and a new developer could navigate it with confidence. The identified outliers are confined to naming convention mismatches — primarily on RelationConfig and ExploreResponseBuilder — rather than layout or export pattern issues. Against a 80-anchor, this codebase sits slightly below at 77 because the RelationConfig naming inconsistency (three conventions on one class) affects a highly-referenced type in the config subsystem.

strengths: Core algorithmic components (CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, SemanticFieldExplorer) have dedicated unit tests using stub executors — testing the algorithm independently of the index
HttpApiUtils and WrapHandler are tested at the utility level — error mapping behavior is covered
GrammarConfigLoader and RelationUtils tests cover configuration loading, which is a critical bootstrap path
No snapshot test overuse — tests use explicit assertions throughout
test_strategy.issue_character: The gap is at the integration level: unit tests are thorough for algorithmic components but the codebase has no integration or end-to-end tests covering the full request-to-response pipeline, and the critical indexing path is untested.
test_strategy.score_rationale: Against a 60-anchor for 'significant drag', this codebase sits above that for unit test quality but below 80 for overall strategy. The unit test suite covers the algorithmic core well using stub-based isolation. However, the empty e2e/ and regression/ directories signal a known gap: there is no verification that the full system works end-to-end, and no regression safety net for the indexer. The BlackLabQueryExecutor — the single most complex class, sitting at the boundary between the application and the BlackLab library — has no integration tests against a real index, meaning subtle query behavior differences are only discoverable in production.

strengths: QueryExecutor parameter ordering is consistent: lemma always first, pattern/deprel second, minLogDice third, maxResults fourth
ExploreOptions bundles variable tuning params into a single object, making exploreByPattern and exploreMultiSeed call sites cleaner and consistent
HTTP endpoint 'top' parameter is uniformly named across all exploration endpoints after the limit→top migration
Error contracts are explicit throughout: all QueryExecutor methods declare throws IOException; all handler validation errors use HttpApiUtils.sendError with consistent HTTP status codes
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The API surface has two coherence gaps: compareCollocateProfiles accepts raw parameters while its sibling methods accept ExploreOptions, and the deprecated 5-param exploreMultiSeed overload adds noise to the public API alongside the preferred ExploreOptions version.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The QueryExecutor interface and handler layer are well-shaped: consistent parameter ordering, uniform error contracts, and clean use of value objects (ExploreOptions) for tuning params. The main coherence gap is in SemanticFieldExplorer's public API: compareCollocateProfiles uses raw (Set, double, int) while explore methods use ExploreOptions, and a deprecated 5-param overload of exploreMultiSeed remains public. Against a 80-anchor for 'solid but uneven', this codebase sits just below at 77 because the API inconsistency is on a public class with multiple callers.

strengths: Loopback-only binding (InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress()) provides network-level isolation without any application-level auth overhead — appropriate for a local tool
CQL injection is consistently mitigated: all user-provided lemmas pass through CqlUtils.escapeForRegex before being embedded in queries
CORS enforcement is centralized in HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader and called by every response method — no endpoint can accidentally miss it
The BCQL endpoint has the most generous attack surface and correctly applies the strictest input validation (length, complexity, body size limits)
authorization_consistency.issue_character: There are no inconsistent authorization patterns because there is no authorization system — this is a consistently unauthenticated local service, which is an appropriate design for its stated deployment context.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: This is a read-only local research server that intentionally has no authentication. The dimension rubric asks for consistency, not for the presence of auth. Within that context, the server is highly consistent: all endpoints have the same (absent) auth model, input validation is uniformly applied, CORS is centrally enforced, and the BCQL endpoint correctly receives extra validation commensurate with its risk. The one gap that keeps the score from 90+ is the absence of rate limiting on the BCQL endpoint, which could be expensive against large corpora.

strengths: Phase decomposition pattern is consistently applied in the most complex algorithms: SemanticFieldExplorer's 4-phase exploration and MultiSeedExplorer's 4-phase multi-seed both use named private helpers with clear, bounded responsibilities
Facade delegation pattern in SemanticFieldExplorer is clean and explicitly documented — the class does not mix algorithmic concerns with the objects it delegates to
Value objects (ExploreOptions, ValidatedExploreRequest, ExploreParams) reduce parameter list length and prevent positional argument confusion at the three most call-heavy code paths
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries successfully eliminates duplicated relation-iteration loops that existed before the BiFunction callback refactoring
design_coherence.issue_character: Design issues are not systemic but rather two localized cross-cutting concerns: ExploreOptions bundles a single-seed-only parameter (nounsPerCollocate) alongside shared params, and compareCollocateProfiles was not included in the ExploreOptions migration, creating a split calling convention within one class.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates solid structural thinking: phase decomposition, facade delegation, and value-object parameter bundling are all applied consistently where they matter most. The identified design gaps are real but localized: the ExploreOptions/raw-params split in SemanticFieldExplorer and the nounsPerCollocate leakage into the shared options object. These affect a moderately important code path but do not represent a systemic design failure. Against a 80-anchor for 'solid but uneven', the codebase sits just below at 79 — the good design decisions outweigh the rough edges by a meaningful margin.

issues.summary: api/ package mixes HTTP handlers, response translation, and rendering in one flat namespace
issues.suggestion: Extract a presentation/ or response/ sub-package for ExploreResponseBuilder and RadialPlot. HTTP handler classes remain in api/. This separates transport from presentation and reduces blast radius for changes to either concern.
evidence: api/ holds 10 files: 5 HTTP handlers (Sketch, Concordance, Bcql, Exploration, Visualization), 1 server (WordSketchApiServer), 1 HTTP utility (HttpApiUtils), 1 response builder (ExploreResponseBuilder), 1 rendering class (RadialPlot), 1 route-wiring seam.
RadialPlot generates SVG output and has no HTTP-specific logic; it is a rendering component.
ExploreResponseBuilder translates model objects to JSON maps — presentation logic, not transport.

issues.summary: RadialPlot (SVG renderer) lives in api/ but has no HTTP-specific logic
issues.suggestion: Move RadialPlot to a visualization/ package (or api/viz/). VisualizationHandlers stays in api/. The rendering logic is then correctly separated from the HTTP adapter.
evidence: RadialPlot.java contains only SVG generation logic with no HttpExchange, no headers, no status codes.
VisualizationHandlers.java is the HTTP adapter that calls RadialPlot.renderFromItems(...) and sends the response — a clean two-level split exists but both live in api/.
A new engineer reading api/ would expect HTTP handlers; finding an SVG layout class with spiral constants breaks that expectation.

issues.summary: QueryResults lives in model/ but belongs conceptually to the query/ package
issues.suggestion: Move QueryResults.java to the query/ package alongside QueryExecutor. Update imports in api/ and exploration/. This aligns production origin with package membership.
evidence: QueryResults is returned by all QueryExecutor methods; it is the output type of the query layer.
model/ is documented as a 'domain model' package, but QueryResults (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, SnippetResult) are corpus-query output structures, not domain concepts.
Moving QueryResults to query/ would make the QueryExecutor interface self-contained and would clarify that model/ holds only semantic-domain types.

issues.summary: Deprecated no-arg-config constructors create live bypass code paths that parallel the config-driven path
issues.suggestion: If the deprecated constructors are only used by legacy tests, annotate them @VisibleForTesting and restrict to test scope. If no test uses them, delete them now rather than waiting for v2.0.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) falls back to FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT = '[xpos="NN.*"]' — a hardcoded regex string bypassing all config logic.
CollocateProfileComparator(QueryExecutor) falls back to FALLBACK_ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = '[xpos="JJ.*"]' — same pattern.
Both have @Deprecated with 'Scheduled for removal in v2.0' but are still compiled and callable.

issues.summary: Deprecated 5-param exploreMultiSeed() is a pure pass-through with no added logic
issues.suggestion: Delete the deprecated 5-param overload. Any caller should migrate to ExploreOptions, which was the stated purpose of the deprecation.
evidence: exploreMultiSeed(Set, RelationConfig, double, int, int) body: 'return multiSeedExplorer.explore(seeds, relationConfig, minLogDice, topCollocates, minShared);'

The preferred overload exploreMultiSeed(Set, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) also delegates directly: 'return multiSeedExplorer.explore(seeds, relationConfig, opts.minLogDice(), opts.topCollocates(), opts.minShared());'
Both are pass-throughs; the 5-param variant adds no policy.

issues.summary: fastjson2 has a lower security-audit track record than mainstream Java JSON libraries
issues.suggestion: Consider migrating to Jackson (com.fasterxml.jackson) or Gson for user-facing request body parsing. If fastjson2 is retained, ensure regular dependency version bumps and monitor its CVE feed.
evidence: fastjson2 is used for JSON parsing of user-supplied request bodies (BcqlHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) and for config loading (GrammarConfigLoader).
fastjson v1 had multiple critical CVEs (e.g. CVE-2022-25845). fastjson2 is architecturally different but carries reputation risk and receives fewer independent security audits than Jackson Databind.
JSON is parsed from untrusted HTTP POST bodies — an audit-risky surface.

issues.summary: parseDoubleParam returns -1.0 as error sentinel, conflating error with potentially valid negative threshold input
issues.suggestion: Throw IllegalArgumentException directly from parseDoubleParam (caught by wrapWithErrorHandling → 400) or use Optional<Double>. Eliminate the -1.0 sentinel entirely.
evidence: parseDoubleParam returns -1.0 when NumberFormatException occurs.
resolveExploreParams checks 'if (minLogDice < 0) return null' — any negative value triggers abort.
The logDice formula is bounded 0–14 so negative values are logically invalid, but the contract is implicit rather than enforced by type.

issues.summary: parseIntParam/parseDoubleParam are private to ExplorationHandlers but needed by ConcordanceHandlers, causing duplication
issues.suggestion: Move parseIntParam and parseDoubleParam to HttpApiUtils as package-accessible static methods. ConcordanceHandlers and any future handler can then use the shared helpers.
evidence: ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples has its own inline try { top = Integer.parseInt(...) } catch (NumberFormatException e) { sendError(400, ...) }.
ExplorationHandlers.parseIntParam implements the identical pattern but is private.
The two implementations send different error messages for the same failure mode.

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition walks tokens via splitCqlTokens then separately scans the raw string with a cursor
issues.suggestion: Refactor to a single pass: extend splitCqlTokens to optionally return offset information, or scan the token list for a '1:' / '2:' prefix directly without re-walking the raw string.
evidence: deriveTokenPosition calls CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens(pattern) to get the token list, then separately scans pattern character-by-character with a cursor variable to find the labeled prefix.
The cursor advance (while cursor < pattern.length() && pattern.charAt(cursor) != '[') duplicates bracket-scanning logic already inside splitCqlTokens.
The method is 20+ lines of interleaved index arithmetic for a conceptually simple 'find labeled token index' operation.

issues.summary: Numeric param parsing helpers are scoped to ExplorationHandlers but leaked as inline duplicates into sibling handler classes
issues.suggestion: Add static parseIntParam(HttpExchange, Map<String,String>, String, int) and parseDoubleParam variants to HttpApiUtils (package-visible). Remove private versions from ExplorationHandlers. ConcordanceHandlers replaces inline try/catch with the shared helper.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.parseIntParam and parseDoubleParam are private methods.

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples contains: 'try { top = Integer.parseInt(params.getOrDefault("top", "10")); } catch (NumberFormatException e) { sendError(400, ...) }' — identical logic, different error message.
The natural home for these helpers is HttpApiUtils, which is already the shared HTTP utility boundary used by all handlers.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison partially re-implements parameter resolution rather than using the shared validated-request preamble
issues.suggestion: Introduce a lighter-weight ValidatedComparisonRequest record (seeds, exploreParams) and a corresponding validateComparisonRequest() method that handles the comparison-specific preamble. This gives handleSemanticFieldComparison its own clean preamble without forcing it to reuse the full exploration flow.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both call validateExploreRequest() which bundles seeds, relation, and params.

handleSemanticFieldComparison calls parseBaseExploreRequest() + requireParam('seeds') + resolveExploreParams() separately — it cannot use validateExploreRequest because it doesn't need a RelationConfig.
The resulting inline code is structurally similar but not identical to the validated-request path, creating slight divergence in error responses.

issues.summary: Two exploration routes log 'Received request' at INFO but nine other routes do not — inconsistent low-value logging
issues.suggestion: Remove the two inline logger.info lines. If per-request logging is needed, add it uniformly in wrapWithErrorHandling (which touches every handler), or use an access log filter.
evidence: registerRoutes() contains: 'registerGetHandler(server, "/api/semantic-field/explore", wrapHandler(exchange -> { logger.info("Received request: {}", exchange.getRequestURI()); explorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore(exchange); }, ...))'
The identical pattern appears for /api/semantic-field/explore-multi.
All other 9 routes (sketch, relations, comparison, concordance, bcql, radial, health) have no per-request info log.
This selective logging provides no diagnostic advantage and pollutes prod logs when these endpoints are called frequently.

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers uses manual if-null-throw while WordSketchApiServer uses Objects.requireNonNull for equivalent guards
issues.suggestion: Replace the manual if-null-throw in ExplorationHandlers with Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig, "...") to match the established pattern.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers constructor: 'if (grammarConfig == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("grammarConfig must not be null; exploration endpoints require a loaded grammar configuration"); }'
WordSketchApiServer constructor: 'this.grammarConfig = Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig, "grammarConfig must not be null");'
Both guard the same type of precondition; the manual form is more verbose and inconsistent.

issues.summary: Three deprecated items in the exploration subsystem are scheduled for 'v2.0' removal with no concrete timeline
issues.suggestion: Grep for callers of the three deprecated methods/constructors. If none exist outside test code, delete them now rather than waiting for v2.0. If test-only, mark @VisibleForTesting and restrict to test scope or replace tests with the new API.
evidence: @Deprecated on SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor): 'Scheduled for removal in v2.0.'
@Deprecated on CollocateProfileComparator(QueryExecutor): 'Scheduled for removal in v2.0.'
@Deprecated on exploreMultiSeed(Set, RelationConfig, double, int, int): 'Scheduled for removal in v2.0.'
